SEED LAUNCH MODULE 5
MOVING FORWARD – PART 1
Welcome to Module Five of Seed Launch Deep Dive Training. I’m very excited about
this call, this is going to be most colorful presentation yet.
Thank you for showing up. Congratulations.
When I first taught Product Launch Formula as a seed launch, I probably started
delivering it in April and then I just kept on delivering more and more content, and I had
some guests on and I started lining up all these guests of people who helped with
launches and all these big name gurus, we were doing all these calls, and it got to be
early July when I’m doing one of the last calls. I’m doing it on a Friday afternoon in July
and we did the whole big long interview and said, “Anyone have any questions out
there?” and when I opened up the lines there wasn’t a single question. I went and
looked on the control to see who was dialed in, and we were the only two people, it was
only me and the person I was interviewing. We were the only people on the call.
So I’m very sensitive that Friday afternoons might not be the time when most people
show up, but we’ve got a full house. Thank you for coming on. We’re going to rock and
roll.

A couple reminders.
It’s not about perfection. I’ve been saying this over and over. It’s about progress, not
perfection.
You will never ever create a perfect product, you’ll never have a perfect squeeze page
or a perfect offer, you’ll never have a perfect launch. I’ve done dozens and dozens of
launches and every single time I make mistakes. Nothing you do is ever going to be
perfect.
The pursuit of perfect is a device that people use to avoid not doing what they should be
doing with their lives. It’s what they use to avoid stepping out of their comfort zone. It’s
like, “I’ll release this when it’s perfect.” It’s never going to be perfect. It’s an avoidance
mechanism, it’s a tool to keep your life small, it’s a tool to avoid stepping out and taking
a big role in the world. Leave it behind.
I tell you this a recovering perfectionist. Actually, the last couple of weeks I made this a
rather epic decision in my life to leave that behind; I’m no longer a perfectionist. I’m
about getting my message out there.
Obviously, if you look, I have accomplished a few things in my life and I have gotten
over that perfectionism at times, but I haven’t contributed nearly as much as I should
have because I’ve let myself be held back by that wanting to be perfect.
A product that is 80% right that gets released is worth a thousand times more to you
and your clients than any nearly perfect product that doesn’t get done. Those nearly
perfect products that don’t get released do nothing but suck the life energy out of you.
That’s why artists drink themselves to death and commit suicide, because they have
this big thing in them and they never bring it out to the world. If you haven’t read The
War of Art by Steven Pressfield, it’s an amazing book and it’s all about this idea.
There’s a book called Undaunted Courage and it’s the story of Lewis and Clark. It’s
basically Meriwether Lewis’s story. These are two explorers and they were the first
people to cross the continent of what would become the United States and make it to
the Pacific.
No one could figure out how to get across the mountains and make it to the Pacific and
they did it. After they did it they were the toast of the nation, they were incredible
heroes. They could do anything they wanted, they could be governors if they wanted,
the President was appointing commissions, they were the biggest heroes in the world.
Remember…

Pricing is an interesting thing. You’re right, the answer is MSU, and the bigger answer is
to test it.
The interesting thing about pricing is as humans we aren’t very good at judging the
value of many things. I was just talking to Eban Pagan about this recently. Our brains
were bred to be out on the Serengeti and if we were hungry we knew the value of
running down an antelope and killing it. If someone already killed it and there’s a slab of
meat, it was pretty easy to judge the value of that slab of meat.
Our brains were not evolved to say, “What is a home study course worth versus a live
event versus a book?” Our brains have a hard time processing that. So, pricing is a
funny thing and perceived value is a funny thing. In general, I think a live event is really
powerful. For some people it’s less powerful. In some markets it’s less powerful.
You could make an argument that you could absolutely sell the recorded course for just
as much as you sold the live one. Or you could discount it. You could discount it by
20%, you could discount it by 40%, you could discount it by 50%. I wouldn’t go any
more than 50%.

Absolutely, of course you can.
“You said you don’t repeat. This is my first product launch. Would it be okay to
repeat?”
I’m not planning on repeating this one, but yes, feel free to. If you want to repeat your
whole seed launch just because you learned some stuff and you want to add some
stuff, you can do it that way. No worries.
“Would you integrate JVs into this strategy?”
In general not with a seed launch. Maybe with the selling of the product I would. Dory
used a joint venture partner. But in general what I want to do is have a tested launch
process before I ask JVs to mail.
The deal is you want to take care of your JV partner’s list even more than you take care
of your list. And obviously you want to take care of your list. But, you really want to take
care of your partner’s list, because if they mail and they don’t get results, they’re not
going to mail again and they’re going to tell other potential partners. So in general I want
to have a tested process.
The way to do it is you do a seed launch, then you roll out the recorded product to your
list and do a regular launch of that recorded product. Make sure you have a good
launch that converts and then you go to JVs. So you do an internal launch and then JV
launch.
Gay said, “Congratulations to you, Jeff, and your awesome team. It’s been an
amazing experience.”
You’re welcome. I had fun doing this.
Jon asks, “Do you ever use early bird pricing?”
Yes and no. I often raise prices. I’ll launch something at a given price and then I’ll raise
the price afterwards. I’ll usually tell people that I’ll raise the price afterwards. But, you
can do whatever you want with the pricing.

Generally within a given launch I don’t love the idea of doing a lower price at the
beginning and raising the price. I think you end up losing people that say, “Why should I
pay$197 when other people paid $147?” I generally avoid doing that.
Question from Julie, “A JV launch is simply approaching JVs after you’ve done a
seed launch so that you have experience?”
Yes, it is. But, JV launches are complicated and they’re a lot of work. You could take all
the work you do with your seed launch, if the amount of work for that is X, the amount of
work for doing a JV launch is two times X. It really does make things harder and more
complicated.
Question from Lori, “What if you don’t get enough buyers? Should you cancel?”
I’ll answer that in a minute with another question coming up.
“Can the next product after the seed launch be very different from the first
product?”
Yes, it can. I wouldn’t want to do a product about six pack abs after I did a learn guitar
product. But, if you did a learn guitar product, you could do a learn blues guitar or you
could do learn how to sheet music product. You want it in the same ballpark.
When I went from teaching about the stock market to teaching about online marketing, I
didn’t mail my old list. That old list I just left behind, I went and created a new list.
“Will you spend time creating new bribes, optimizing list building, or do you just
keep the original bribe created for the seed list?” Marco asked.
Basically it’s just about testing. You’re always testing your opt-in page. The thing to test
on there is your bribes and your headlines. So, I’m always testing, I’m always trying to
make it better.
The squeeze page doesn’t necessarily relate to your seed launch straight up. It could
just be if you’re teaching about guitar your bribe is about learning guitar, it doesn’t have
to be directly related to playing blues lead guitar.
It’s just whatever bribe is working the best for you at a given time. I’m always testing.

This one came in on the Module Five survey and it says, “What if you only get six
people to sign up once you launch? Should you do the program? It would be
pretty tough to comp 24 people in.”
That was an interesting question. I know someone with a seed launch who did this, who
basically rolled out the seed launch and only sold six. In fact, in the case study with Tara
Marino she sold six. It might have been five, I can’t remember if it was five or six. She
said, “This is in me and I’ve got to do it, I have to teach this stuff.” And so she did.
In this case, the person I’m thinking of that did this rolled out their seed launch and sold
six of them. He sold six and he needed the money so he decided, “I’m going to do this. I
made this amount of money and I have to get moving.” So he comped in not just 24
people, but more like 34 people and brought the total up to about 40.
He went through and delivered his product. He had never taught about this product
before, it was a whole different business for him. He went through and taught, and as he
went through the seed launch he just kept on getting bigger and bigger, but he gradually
learned how to teach that product. He learned how to teach within that whole new
market.
Sure enough, it worked just like it was supposed to and he ended up creating the home
study course out of that. Actually, he was a perfectionist so the recordings of the calls
weren’t good enough for him, so he actually went and re-did the whole thing as a whole
bunch of videos. But he basically took what he learned in the initial teaching of that seed
launch. Even though he only sold six of them and he had to comp in 34 people.
It would seem like it was odd to have six times as many people comped in as paying,
but the thing is when you comp someone in you just say, “Please don’t share. I’m happy
to do this for you, but a lot of people paid money for this so don’t go in there and tell
everyone.”

I just have one final thought here. This is a quote that I absolutely love. I know many of
you have seen this and many of you are familiar with this.
It’s from Marianne Williamson. She wrote, “Our deepest fear is not that we are
inadequate. Our deepest fear is that we are powerful beyond measure. It is our light, not
our darkness that most frightens us. We ask ourselves, ‘Who am I to be brilliant,
gorgeous, talented, fabulous?’ Actually, who are you not to be? You’re a child of God.
Your playing small does not serve the world. There is nothing enlightened about
shrinking so that other people won’t feel insecure around you. We are all meant to
shine, as children do. We are born to manifest the glory of God that is within us. It is not
just in some of us, it’s in everyone. As we let our light shine, we unconsciously give
other people permission to do the same. As we are liberated from our own fear, our
presence automatically liberates others.”
So who are you to hold yourself back? Who are you to play small?
